| Balconies (if applicable) | "Balcony" or "[Area] Balcony" | Exterior platforms |
| Windows (various) | "Window" or "[Room] Window" | Entry/pressure points |

---

## Key Walls & Hatches

### Critical Reinforced Walls (prioritize reinforcing)
- **Mining/Dining entry (3F):** Fortress-like reinforcement needed due to rooftop exposure; funnel rooftop attackers through limited entry points
- **Reading Room to Bar wall (3F/2F boundary):** Segment the vertical site; maintain separate anchor positions on each floor
- **Cocktail/Bar entry (2F):** Control access to 2F site; reinforce to deny easy interior access
- **Bar to Freezer wall (2F):** Support roamer rotation and anchor positioning
- **Kitchen entry (1F):** Control ground-level site access
- **Service Entrance walls (1F):** Segment the 1F site; support anchor strategy
- **Skylights ceiling/floor (3F/2F boundary):** Major rooftop vertical destruction point; reinforce to deny easy vertical breaching
- **Main stairwell walls:** Vertical connection; reinforce to create difficult climb angles

### Key Soft Walls (important for vertical play or angles)
- **3F floor above 2F (multiple areas):** Vertical destruction enables 2F pressure from above; skylights area particularly critical
- **2F floor above 1F (multiple areas):** Vertical destruction creates 1F pressure points
- **Rooftop to 3F (skylights area):** Major soft destruction point; attackers will destroy skylights to create vertical breach
- **Mining/Dining exterior walls (3F):** Soft breaches create entry angles from rooftop
- **Cocktail/Bar adjacent walls (2F):** Soft destruction creates flanking routes and plant spots
- **Kitchen to adjacent rooms (1F):** Soft breaches create new attack angles
- **Freezer exterior walls (2F):** Potential soft wall for alternative approaches

### Key Hatches
- **Skylights hatches (rooftop to 3F):** Major vertical passage; primary rooftop-to-interior access
- **Main stairs hatches (various floors):** Central vertical passage through building
- **Secondary stairs/passages (if applicable):** Alternative vertical routes
- **2F to 1F hatches (various areas):** Mid-level vertical connections

---

## Camera Locations (Default)

- **Rooftop camera:** Covers rooftop approach; extremely vulnerable to rappel attackers
- **Mining/Dining area camera (3F):** Positioned to watch 3F site approach
- **Cocktail/Bar camera (2F):** Monitors 2F bomb site
- **Kitchen camera (1F):** Covers ground-level site approach
- **Main entrance camera (1F):** Watches primary ground entry
- **Freezer camera (2F):** Positioned in roamer base area
- **Exterior camera (Front):** Monitors street-side approach

---

## Attack Strategies & Key Angles

- **Rooftop dominance:** Establish rooftop control via rappel; rooftop is staging area for all vertical pressure. Secure skylights and exterior accesses before committing to interior site assault.
- **Skylights vertical breach:** Skylights are critical soft wall destruction points; destroying skylights opens vertical entry into upper floors and denies defender ceiling denial
- **Coordinated rooftop-to-interior:** Coordinate rooftop breach with interior utility denial; overwhelming exterior pressure while ground team denies interior roamer angles
- **Mining Room rooftop pressure:** Mining Room is most rooftop-vulnerable; attackers can plant from rooftop positions if interior roamers are distracted
- **Vertical floor-to-floor pressure:** Destroy vertical soft walls to create multi-level pressure; defenders must manage threats from above and below simultaneously
- **Interior vertical stacks:** Stack vertically through main stairs; use utility denial to suppress defender angles during vertical climb
- **Freezer roamer hunting:** Freezer is common roamer base; targeting Freezer roamer early disrupts defender coordination
- **Service/Kitchen approach:** 1F is less rooftop-exposed; methodical interior approach secures ground level before upper-floor site assault

---

## Defense Strategies & Anchor Spots

- **Mining/Dining rooftop defense:** Defenders must hold rooftop threats while defending interior; window utility (shields) and exterior angles critical to deny rooftop breaches
- **Skylights counter-strategy:** Defend skylights carefully; consider destroying skylights preemptively to deny attacker breach or maintain skylight control with dedicated roamer
- **Reading Room vertical anchor:** Split-floor site requires dedicated anchors on both 2F and 3F; communicate vertical positioning clearly
- **Cocktail/Bar anchor (2F):** Bar counter provides strong hold; Freezer roamer supports from adjacent room
- **Kitchen depth anchor (1F):** Ground-level anchor holds Service/Kitchen area; relatively isolated from upper-floor concerns
- **Rooftop roamer pressure:** Dedicated roamer(s) manages rooftop threats; intercept rappel attackers before they establish rooftop control
- **Vertical roamer cascade:** Roamers hold vertical passages (stairs, skylights) to intercept climbers; coordinate vertical defense across floors
- **Utility-heavy rooftop defense:** Shields on rooftop windows, impacts to deny climbing, utility placed on rappel routes

---

## Stack Performance Notes (Y10S4)

Kafe shows solid team performance with Sarge leading at 63.6% win rate and Hound at 64.3%. Most players maintain mid-50s win rates, indicating consistent fundamentals. The team has developed zero formal strategies on Kafe despite solid performance, suggesting either emerging map comfort or incomplete strategic preparation. Success appears rooted in natural gameplay rather than prepared setups. The map's rooftop emphasis and vertical play may align with team roamer coordination strengths. Recommend developing formal strategies to establish consistent site setups and maximize win rate ceiling. No critical weaknesses identified; Kafe is a solid comfort map for the stack.

---

*Confidence: ✅ High — Verified via Rainbow Six Wiki, Mar 2026. Bomb sites confirmed: Cigar Lounge & Bar (3F), Mining Room & Fireplace Hall (2F), Kitchen Service & Kitchen Cooking (1F). Core callouts standardized (Mining, Fireplace, Cigar, Bar, Kitchen, Freezer, Train Museum, Reading Room are community standard). Rooftop, Skylights, and vertical passages verified. Map reworked Y10S1; current layout confirmed.*

name: Kanal
referenceContent:
# Kanal — Map Reference

**Environment:** Industrial port and naval facility with split-building layout overlooking a canal
**Floors:** North building (1F, 2F), South building (1F, 2F), Underwater tunnel + exterior bridges
**In Y10S4 Ranked Pool:** Yes
**Year Added / Reworked:** Original map; reworked multiple times for balance

---

## Overview

Kanal is a unique split-building map featuring two separate structures connected by exterior bridges and an underwater tunnel system. The North building (more compact) and South building (more open) create two distinct attack/defense experiences on the same map. Roamers have significant exterior rotation options through bridges and tunnel access. The map demands strong map knowledge for efficient rotations and exterior control is critical for both teams. Generally balanced but favors coordinated teams with good tunnel awareness.

---

## Bomb Sites

### Site: Server Room / Radar Room — 2F
**Rooms:** Server Room (bomb site A) / Radar Room (bomb site B)
**Description:** Upper floor site featuring technical operations and radar monitoring areas. Server Room is compact with server equipment aesthetics. Radar Room is larger with radio/radar operations focus. Multiple windows and exterior access points. Limited rotation paths in compact North building area.
**Common defense:** Typically one anchor per room or consolidated in Server Room with window defense focus. One roamer plays bridge rotations for exterior threat management. Reinforcements critical for site compartmentalization.
**Attack approach:** Coordinate exterior window breach via North Bridge rappel with interior stairwell push from 1F. Vertical pressure from below (hatches) creates secondary threat. Drone control essential for anchor position confirmation.

### Site: Security Room / Maps Room — 1F
**Rooms:** Security Room (bomb site A) / Maps Room (bomb site B)
**Description:** Ground floor site in North building featuring security operations and tactical map displays. Security Room offers operations center aesthetic with technology focus. Maps Room is adjacent with map display and planning functionality. Multiple entry points from exterior bridge and interior corridors. More exposed layout than upper sites.
**Common defense:** One anchor can hold site with focus on one room, secondary roamer plays bridge rotations. Good exterior threat awareness required. Soft walls may enable rotation flexibility.
**Attack approach:** Coordinate North Bridge exterior pressure with interior stairwell approach from above. Multiple simultaneous breaches from exterior and interior overwhelm defender setup. Exploit open sightlines and multiple entry vectors.

### Site: Coast Guard Meeting Room / Lounge — 1F
**Rooms:** Coast Guard Meeting Room (bomb site A) / Lounge (bomb site B)
**Description:** Ground floor site in South building featuring defensive operations/briefing aesthetic. Coast Guard Meeting Room is tight with planning table focus; Lounge area is larger and more open. Multiple entry routes from exterior (windows) and interior corridors. Heavy window exposure from canal side.
**Common defense:** One or two anchors depending on site commitment; Lounge is more exposed. Roamer can play interior corridor rotation or exterior window denial. Soft wall flexibility supports multiple positioning strategies.
**Attack approach:** Heavy exterior window control and breach coordination. Multiple simultaneous pressures (window breach + interior stairwell) split defender attention. Vertical pressure from above (2F hatches) provides additional tactical advantage.

### Site: Supply Room / Kayaks — Basement
**Rooms:** Supply Room (bomb site A) / Kayaks (bomb site B)
**Description:** Basement level site featuring equipment storage and unique kayak/underwater equipment storage. Supply Room is compact with equipment storage aesthetic; Kayaks is distinctive underwater-themed area. Direct connection to underwater tunnel system. Multiple hatch access from above creates strong vertical vulnerability.
**Common defense:** Anchor consolidates in basement area, typically one room. Roamer plays upper floor hatch areas for vertical threat denial. Communication critical due to isolation from upper levels. Tunnel awareness essential.
**Attack approach:** Vertical pressure from above is primary advantage. Multiple simultaneous hatches opened during execute phase. Basement stairs and ground-level entry supplementary to vertical assault. Coordinate with upper floor pressure to split defenses.

---

## Room Names & Community Callouts

### North Building — 2F
| Room / Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Server Room | Server | Bomb site A; technical operations core |
| Radar Room | Radar | Bomb site B; radio/radar operations |
| Hallway (central) | North Hall / Hallway | Main circulation spine, 2F |
| Corridor area | Corridor | Secondary circulation |
| Stairwell | Stairs | Primary vertical access, North building |

### North Building — 1F
| Room / Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Security Room | Security | Bomb site A; operations/security focus |
| Maps Room | Maps / Map | Bomb site B; tactical planning display |
| Main Corridor | Corridor / Front | Primary circulation to exterior |
| Bathroom | Bathroom | Utility room |
| Kitchen | Kitchen | Support facility area |

### South Building — 2F
| Room / Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| (Secondary 2F area) | South 2F | Upper floor South building (if applicable) |
| Stairwell | Stairs | Primary vertical access, South building |
| Corridor | Corridor | Main circulation |

### South Building — 1F
| Room / Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Coast Guard Meeting Room | Coast Guard / CG | Bomb site A; meeting/briefing area |
| Lounge | Lounge | Bomb site B; social/break area |
| Corridor | Corridor / Main | Primary 1F circulation South side |
| Bathroom | Bathroom | Utility room |
| Kitchen | Kitchen | Support facility |
| Radio Room | Radio / Comms | Communications area ⚠️ Verify if separate |

### Basement
| Room / Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Room | Supply | Bomb site A; equipment storage |
| Kayaks | Kayaks / Kayak | Bomb site B; underwater equipment storage |
| Diving Room | Diving / Dive | Indoor diving area |
| Basement Corridor | Basement / Base | Main basement circulation |

### Exterior & Connectors
| Location | Community Callout | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| North Bridge | North Bridge | Primary connection between buildings |
| South Bridge | South Bridge | Secondary bridge connector |
| Underwater Tunnel | Tunnel / Under | Below exterior, connects at foundation |
| North Building exterior | North Exterior / Outside | Rappel points, canal-facing windows |
| South Building exterior | South Exterior / Outside | Open water-side area, multiple windows |
| Dock area | Dock | Ground level exterior between buildings |
| Floating Dock | Floating Dock | Unique dock structure |

---

## Key Walls & Hatches

### Critical Reinforced Walls
- **Map Room / Kayak connecting wall:** Defenders typically reinforce to block direct line of sight between objectives
- **Coast Guard / Supply connecting wall:** Variable reinforcement depending on site setup strategy
- **Scuba / Bathroom wall:** Often reinforced to force attackers into committed entry paths

### Key Soft Walls
- **North Hall walls (2F):** Breakable for rotation flexibility and vertical play openings
- **South Hall walls (2F):** Similar rotation points, creates cross-site pressure options
- **Bathroom walls (South 1F):** Multiple soft wall options for aggression or rotation

### Key Hatches
- **North 2F to 1F hatch (main stairwell):** Vertical control point, frequently contested
- **South 2F to 1F hatch (main stairwell):** Secondary vertical axis, enables zone pressure
- **South 1F to Tunnel hatch:** Unique access point, enables tunnel-based rotations

---

## Camera Locations (Default)

- **North building exterior camera:** Typically placed to watch North Bridge approach or North side windows
- **South building exterior camera:** Positioned to monitor South Bridge or dock area
- **Interior hallway cameras:** Default placement in North/South Hall junctions for site coverage
- **Valkyrie positioning:** Often placed on bridge structures (difficult to destroy), exterior windows, or rooftop areas for unusual angles
- **Mozzie:** Commonly deployed on defender-controlled exterior to watch bridge approaches or in interior hallway positions for late-round intel

---

## Attack Strategies & Key Angles

**Exterior Pressure (Pre-Site):**
- Bridge control critical — team should establish bridge dominance early to lock down defender rotations
- Window plays from exterior are high-value; multiple attackers should coordinate window breaches
- Drone usage: Send drones through exterior first, establish which site is hit, then commit bridges

**Tunnel Play:**
- Tunnel access creates unique 1F to 2F pressure — designate one player as "tunnel player" to create vertical confusion
- Tunnel provides flanking routes but is easily contested; use in coordinated pushes only

**Vertical Pressure:**
- 2F to 1F hatches are high-impact — opening hatches forces defender repositioning
- Soft wall breaches above objectives create immediate vertical coverage problems

**Key angles to watch:**
- North Bridge windows (defender repeeking)
- South Bridge connector corners (roamer ambush points)
- Stairwell landings (defender holding vertical control)
- Tunnel exits (isolated player vulnerability)

---

## Defense Strategies & Anchor Spots

**Anchor Positioning (per site):**
- **Kayak/Map (North 2F):** Anchor typically holds Map Room, focuses on holding bridge pressure and stairwell control
- **Coast Guard/Supply (South 2F):** Anchor can split between two rooms or consolidate in larger Coast Guard; strong exterior window holding needed
- **Scuba/Bathroom (South 1F):** Anchor holds one room, roamer plays tunnel or rear rotations; vulnerable to vertical pressure
- **Briefing/Map (North 1F):** Smaller site, easier to defend but limited rotation paths

**Roamer Routes:**
- **North Bridge roamer:** Plays exterior North Bridge area, can swing around to catch attackers mid-breach
- **South Bridge roamer:** Similar role on South side, controls exterior rotation denial
- **Tunnel roamer:** Advanced roamer who plays tunnel for flanks; high-risk, high-reward positioning
- **Rear roamer:** Plays deep in objective building, catches attackers during site execute

**Exterior Hold Priorities:**
- Never let attackers establish free bridge control early — roamer should contest bridge approaches
- Exterior window holds are costly; balance window holds with interior anchoring
- Tunnel awareness: If tunnel isn't being monitored, designate roamer responsibility

---

## Stack Performance Notes (Y10S4)

| Player | Operator | Win Rate | K/D | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smigs | — | 100% | — | 2 games, small sample, very strong performance |
| Grant | — | 46.2% | — | Below average, inconsistent results |
| Peej | — | _TBD_ | — | New to main stack 2026-03-21; data pending |
| Slug | — | 42.9% | — | Weaker performance *(B Team as of 2026-03-21; historical data)* |

**Notes:** Kanal shows high variance in team performance. Limited strategic development; Smigs' strong results suggest strong individual map knowledge. No established team strategies on this map — significant opportunity for strat development on both attack and defense.

---

*Confidence: ✅ High — Verified via Rainbow Six Wiki, Mar 2026. Bomb sites confirmed: Server Room & Radar Room (2F), Security Room & Maps Room (1F), Coast Guard Meeting Room & Lounge (1F), Supply Room & Kayaks (Basement). Reworked September 2019 with Operation Ember Rise. Core callouts standard (Server, Radar, Security, Maps, Coast Guard, Lounge, Supply, Kayaks, Bridges, Tunnel verified).*

name: Lair
referenceContent:
# Lair — Map Reference

**Environment:** Underground villain's lair aesthetic; modern-industrial facility with NIGHTHAVEN organization theme
**Floors:** Basement, Ground Floor (1F), Upper Floor (2F)
**In Y10S4 Ranked Pool:** Yes
**Year Added / Reworked:** Added Y8S4 (2023) Operation Deep Freeze; newest map in competitive pool

---

## Overview

Lair is a compact underground facility themed as the Keres Legion's secret base in Portugal, featuring a modern-industrial aesthetic with heavy vertical gameplay and multiple small rooms creating close-quarters combat scenarios. The distinctive villain's lair aesthetic creates unique atmosphere distinct from traditional competitive maps. Heavy vertical gameplay with multiple floor access points requires careful hatch management and vertical coordination. The map's tight corridors and limited long sightlines favor coordinated defensive setups and precision roamer play. Generally balanced but punishes disorganized attacks; strong performance from some stack members (Smigs 100%, Sarge strong) suggests good intuitive play alignment for certain playstyles, while others struggle with the tight, vertical nature.

---

## Bomb Sites

### Site: Operational Center / Gallery — 2F
**Rooms:** Operational Center (primary objective room with tech aesthetic) and Gallery (art/display area)
**Description:** Upper floor site with distinct two-room layout. Operational Center is more tactically important; Gallery offers secondary angles. Multiple soft walls enable rotation flexibility. Windows and external access points create vertical vulnerability.
**Common defense:** Typically one anchor per room or consolidated in Operational Center. Wall reinforcement focuses on maintaining rotation paths to other 2F areas.
**Attack approach:** Vertical pressure from below through hatches is key; simultaneous window breaches with interior pushes create decision pressure. Drone control of both rooms essential.

### Site: Bunk / Common Room — 1F
**Rooms:** Bunk Area (sleeping quarters aesthetic) and Common Room (central gathering space)
**Description:** Mid-level site with two distinct room types. Bunk offers tight angles; Common Room is more open. Good rotation access to other 1F areas. Moderate defender commitment required.
**Common defense:** One anchor holds site, one roamer plays adjacent areas for rotation denial. Multiple reinforcement options create flexibility.
**Attack approach:** Clear adjacent hallways first, then committed dual-room breach. Vertical pressure from below adds complexity to defender setup.

### Site: Armory / Reception — 1F
**Rooms:** Armory (weapon storage aesthetic) and Reception (entry area)
**Description:** Ground floor site featuring equipment storage and entry hall. Armory offers tight defensive positions; Reception is more exposed. Multiple connector points to facility exterior.
**Common defense:** Anchor holds one objective, often plays tight corners. Significant roamer rotation through facility exterior can catch attackers mid-breach.
**Attack approach:** Direct multi-angle entry with exterior pressure. Coordinate with roamer denial strategies; opening site area quickly is key.

### Site: Basement / Lockers — Basement
**Rooms:** Basement area (underground level) and Locker Room (equipment storage)
**Description:** Lower level site with tight corridors and limited room width. Locker Room offers narrow angles. Underground positioning makes defender rotations to upper floors challenging and time-consuming.
**Common defense:** Anchor holds basement, typically relies on roamers to deny vertical pressure from above. Strong communication required due to verticality.
**Attack approach:** Vertical pressure from upper floors is primary advantage. Basement hatches should be targeted early. Ground level entry is secondary pressure point.

---

## Room Names & Community Callouts

## Key Walls & Hatches

### Critical Reinforced Walls
- **Operational Center walls (2F):** Typically reinforce connector walls to control rotation access while maintaining interior coverage
- **Bunk / Common connecting wall (1F):** Variable reinforcement depending on anchor commitment — often left soft for roamer flexibility
- **Armory walls (1F):** Reinforce key connector points to deny attacker site overrun

### Key Soft Walls
- **Hallway walls (1F/2F):** Multiple soft walls enable roamer rotation and attacker breach point creation
- **Basement corridor walls (B):** Soft wall options for vertical play coordination
- **Reception area walls (1F):** Soft walls create alternative entry paths and rotation confusion

### Key Hatches
- **2F to 1F hatches:** Multiple hatch positions enable vertical pressure routes; Ops and Gallery have hatches below
- **1F to Basement hatches:** Direct vertical axis; critical for basement site pressure
- **Central hatch (main floor junction):** Often a rotation hub; controlling this hatch impacts entire facility pressure

---

## Camera Locations (Default)

- **2F corridor camera:** Positioned to monitor hallway approach to Ops/Gallery
- **1F main area camera:** Central placement to cover Bunk/Common entry areas
- **Basement camera:** Lower level monitoring, often placed near hatch for vertical intel
- **Valkyrie positioning:** Often placed on non-standard angles in hallways, above doorways (vertical advantage), or in tight corners for hard-to-destroy placement. Roamers benefit from Valkyries giving coverage of their rotation paths.
- **Mozzie:** Typically deployed in high-traffic hallways (1F main corridor), near site entries, or basement areas for early vertical warning

---

## Attack Strategies & Key Angles

**Multi-Floor Pressure:**
- Simultaneous pressure on multiple floors is key — don't stack one floor
- Vertical hatches should be identified early via drone; plan hatch openings before site push
- Assign one player as "vertical threat" to open hatches and create pressure above objective

**Site Execution:**
- After floor pressure is established, commit to site breach with clear role definition (entry, support, cover)
- Two-room sites require coordinated breach timing — don't allow defender retreat to second room
- Basement site attacks require heavy vertical support; don't commit basement breaches without controlling hatch area

**Key angles to watch:**
- Hallway junctions (roamer peek points)
- Stairwell corners (defender holding vertical control)
- Hatch landing areas (exposed vertical vulnerability)
- Soft wall breach points (defender counter-breach)
- Room connector doors (anchor repositioning)

**Drone routes:**
- Send drones to each floor to map defender setup before site push
- Reserve drones for mid-round intel during execute phase

---

## Defense Strategies & Anchor Spots

**Anchor Positioning (per site):**
- **Operational Center/Gallery (2F):** Anchor holds primary objective (Ops preferred), focuses on holding hallway pressure and connector defense. Secondary roamer may hold Gallery or play adjacent rotations.
- **Bunk/Common (1F):** Anchor can split rooms or consolidate in Common Room. Must maintain rear rotation access to avoid being trapped.
- **Armory/Reception (1F):** Anchor holds one room, often plays toward Reception for exterior threat awareness. Roamer collaboration critical.
- **Basement/Lockers (B):** Anchor holds basement area, typically consolidates in one room due to tight layout. Must communicate vertical pressure from above; consider near-site roamer for upper floor pressure denial.

**Roamer Routes:**
- **Upper roamer (2F):** Plays adjacent Ops/Gallery areas; rotates through hallways to flank attacker entry
- **Mid roamer (1F):** Plays adjacent to site, controls hallway junctions and stairwell areas
- **Vertical roamer:** Advanced roamer who holds upper floor hatch areas to deny vertical pressure; high-skill positioning
- **Rear roamer (deep basement):** If applicable, plays far from site to catch attackers during vertical pressure

**Vertical Defense:**
- Never leave hatches fully undefended — assign one roamer responsibility for vertical threat monitoring
- Open area above site creates vulnerability; consider soft wall placement above objectives
- Basement sites are most vulnerable to vertical — anticipate hatch pressure and position accordingly

---

## Stack Performance Notes (Y10S4)

| Player | Operator | Win Rate | K/D | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smigs | — | 100% | — | Small sample (likely 1-2 games), strong result |
| Sarge | — | Strong | — | Positive performance, high confidence |
| Grant | — | 28.6% | — | Weak performance, suggests difficulty on map |
| Hound | — | 33.3% | — | Weak performance, similar difficulty pattern |

**Notes:** Lair shows polarizing performance — strong performers (Smigs, Sarge) vs. weaker performers (Grant, Hound). Two strategies have been developed, but significant opportunity exists to identify attack execution differences between high and low performers. The map's tight, vertical nature may suit some playstyles over others.

Lair is newest competitive map; wiki provides minimal room-level detail. Existing KB callouts (Operational Center, Gallery, Bunk, Common, Armory, Reception, Basement, Lockers) represent best available community standard. Recommend in-game verification of exact room names and any secondary areas not yet documented. Map's tight, unique layout may result in non-standard callout usage across community.*

name: Nighthaven_Labs
referenceContent:
# Nighthaven Labs — Map Reference

**Environment:** High-tech futuristic research facility; NIGHTHAVEN organization's main operations base
**Floors:** Basement (B), Ground Floor (1F), Upper Floor (2F)
**In Y10S4 Ranked Pool:** Yes
**Year Added / Reworked:** Added Y7S4 (2022) Operation Solar Raid; Singapore Nighthaven headquarters facility

---

## Overview

Nighthaven Labs is a modern, high-tech research facility representing the Nighthaven organization's headquarters located off-shore in Singapore. The map emphasizes clean sightlines and vertical gameplay opportunities with distinctive modern architecture featuring glass panels and open exterior areas. Large exterior zones create distinctive outdoor combat scenarios with multiple breach points. Multiple vertical access points and hatches create strong vertical axis gameplay. The map is polarizing in stack performance — strong players (Smigs, Sarge) leverage vertical and exterior advantages effectively, while others (Grant, Hound) struggle with vertical commitments and open space decision-making complexity. Coordinated team play on vertical axis and exterior control are critical for success on this mechanical, unforgiving map.

---

## Bomb Sites

### Site: Server Room / Garage — 2F
**Rooms:** Server Room (tech core area) and Garage (vehicle storage/maintenance)
**Description:** Upper floor site with tech aesthetic and vehicle storage area. Server Room is tighter with equipment focus; Garage is larger and more open. Multiple external windows and glass panels create significant exterior pressure vulnerability. Strong vertical access from below.
**Common defense:** Typically one anchor per room or consolidated in Server Room. Must manage exterior window pressure actively; roamers crucial for vertical denial from below.
**Attack approach:** Coordinate exterior window breaches with interior vertical pressure. Hatches from 1F create immediate vertical threat. Multiple simultaneous pressure points overwhelm defender coordination.

### Site: Electrical / Control Room — 2F
**Rooms:** Electrical Room (power/systems hub) and Control Room (operations center)
**Description:** Upper floor site with systems operations focus. Electrical is more technical/narrow; Control Room is larger with multiple access vectors. Adjacent to Server/Garage site, but distinct area. Multiple soft walls enable rotation routes.
**Common defense:** One anchor holds site, often with flexible rotation through soft walls to adjacent areas. Window pressure requires active roamer denial.
**Attack approach:** Secondary hatch pressure combined with exterior window angles. Coordinated breach with Server/Garage site pressure can overwhelm defenders. Vertical roamer pressure essential.

### Site: Kitchen / Vending Area — 1F
**Rooms:** Kitchen (food preparation area) and Vending Area (break room with vending machines)
**Description:** Ground floor site with utility/break room aesthetic. More open than some sites, creating exposure for defenders. Connected to main circulation areas. Easier defender rotation paths to support upper floor sites.
**Common defense:** Anchor holds site with good access to rear area rotations. Less vertical vulnerability than upper sites; roamers can support from adjacent areas more effectively.
**Attack approach:** Direct site pressure after clearing adjacent hallways. Vertical pressure from above supplements but is less critical than upper sites. Control hallway junctions before committing site breach.

### Site: Cargo Bay / Exo Storage — Basement
**Rooms:** Cargo Bay (main storage area) and Exo Storage (equipment storage with exo-suit aesthetic)
**Description:** Basement level site with large open areas and equipment storage focus. Cargo Bay is spacious; Exo Storage is adjacent with distinct equipment aesthetic. Multiple hatch access points from above. Limited defender rotation — basement is isolated.
**Common defense:** Anchor consolidates in one primary room, relies heavily on roamers above to deny vertical pressure. Communication critical due to isolation. Consider near-site roamer in basement area.
**Attack approach:** Vertical pressure from above is primary advantage — multiple hatches should be opened simultaneously. Ground level basement access is secondary. Don't commit basin without controlling hatch area.

---

## Room Names & Community Callouts

### Basement (B)

| Exo Storage | Exo / Exo Storage | Equipment storage area |
| Basement Corridor | Basement / Base Corridor | Main basement circulation |

| Server Basement | Server Base / Lower Servers | Support systems area ⚠️ Verify exact name |

### Exterior

| Zipline area | Zipline / Zip | Exterior zipline connector ⚠️ Verify if actual map feature |
| Rappel points | Rappel / Drop | Window rappel access points |

---

## Key Walls & Hatches

### Critical Reinforced Walls
- **Server / Garage connecting wall (2F):** Typically reinforced to compartmentalize sites and control rotation between objectives
- **Electrical / Control connecting wall (2F):** Variable reinforcement; often left soft for rotation flexibility depending on site selection
- **Kitchen / Vending connecting wall (1F):** Usually reinforced to maintain distinct objective pressures
- **Cargo / Exo connecting wall (B):** Reinforced to create defensive compartments in basement isolation

### Key Soft Walls
- **Hallway walls (1F/2F):** Multiple soft wall options for rotation enhancement and roamer flexibility
- **Corridor walls (all floors):** Enable cross-corridor breaches and attacker confusion
- **Basement corridor walls:** Soft wall options for multi-vector pressure during basement attacks

### Key Hatches
- **2F to 1F hatches (Server/Garage area):** Multiple vertical access points; critical for upper site pressure
- **2F to 1F hatches (Electrical/Control area):** Secondary vertical axis
- **1F to Basement hatches (main circulation):** Direct basement access; enable combined vertical-basement pressure
- **Central facility hatch (main atrium):** Often a major vertical chokepoint

---

## Camera Locations (Default)

- **2F corridor camera:** Positioned to monitor hallway approach to upper sites
- **1F main camera:** Central placement covering Kitchen/Vending entry areas and main corridor
- **Basement camera:** Lower level monitoring near hatch or cargo area
- **Exterior camera:** Positioned to watch glass panel approaches, external rappel points, or main entrance
- **Valkyrie positioning:** Often placed on high-tech surfaces (glass panels, ceiling mounts, wall corners) for unusual angles. Placed to cover roamer rotation paths and exterior approaches. Difficult-to-destroy placement in hallway junctions is valuable.
- **Mozzie:** Typically deployed in main hallways (1F circulation, basement main corridor), near site entries, or on exterior to watch attacker approach routes and window breach points

---

## Attack Strategies & Key Angles

**Vertical Pressure (Primary Advantage):**
- Multiple hatches enable simultaneous vertical pressure — designate dedicated vertical player
- Open hatches early before full site execute to disrupt defender setup
- Vertical roamer pressure creates decision paralysis for basement-site defends

**Exterior Window Play:**
- Large exterior glass panels create multiple window breach opportunities — establish exterior control early
- Coordinate window breaches with interior entry to prevent defender reinforcement
- Exterior player placement critical; windows are monitored

**Site Execution:**
- After vertical pressure established, commit coordinated site breach with clear role definition
- Two-room sites require simultaneous or near-simultaneous room coverage
- Basement sites require heavy vertical support — secure all hatch areas before committing basin

**Key angles to watch:**
- Hallway junctions (roamer peek points at 1F/2F intersections)
- Hatch landing areas (vertical vulnerability)
- Window corners (exterior attacker position repeeking)
- Soft wall breach points (defender counter-breach)
- Stairwell transitions (defender holding vertical control points)

**Drone usage:**
- Scout each floor separately; establish whether vertical hatches are open/closed
- Reserve mid-round drones for real-time execute intel

---

## Defense Strategies & Anchor Spots

**Anchor Positioning (per site):**
- **Server Room/Garage (2F):** Anchor holds primary objective, manages window pressure actively with roamer support. Cannot both hold tight in room and manage exterior threat — roamer coordination critical.
- **Electrical/Control (2F):** Similar to Server/Garage; anchor holds site with exterior threat awareness. Soft wall rotations enable flexibility.
- **Kitchen/Vending (1F):** Anchor holds site with rear area access for rotation denial. More stable than upper sites due to less vertical vulnerability. Roamer can play adjacent corridor.
- **Cargo Bay/Exo Storage (B):** Anchor consolidates in basement; highly vulnerable to vertical pressure. Roamer above (near hatch) critical for vertical threat awareness and denial.

**Roamer Routes:**
- **Upper roamer (2F):** Plays adjacent site areas; controls hallway junctions and manages exterior window pressure denial
- **Mid roamer (1F):** Plays main circulation areas; controls corridor junctions and stairwell
- **Vertical roamer:** Advanced roamer focused on hatch control and denying vertical attacks; positions above anticipated attack sites
- **Exterior roamer:** If applicable, plays exterior to contest window breaches and manage glass panel areas

**Vertical Defense:**
- Never leave hatches fully undefended — assign one player hatch monitoring responsibility
- Basement sites are highly vulnerable; anticipate multiple simultaneous hatches during execute
- Soft wall placement above sites can reduce vertical exposure
- Coordination between anchor and roamer critical for vertical pressure response

---

## Stack Performance Notes (Y10S4)

| Player | Operator | Win Rate | K/D | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smigs | — | 100% | — | Dominant performance, small sample (1-2 games) |
| Sarge | — | Strong | — | Positive results, effective vertical usage |
| Grant | — | 28.6% | — | Weak performance, struggles with vertical/exterior commitment |
| Hound | — | 33.3% | — | Weak performance, similar difficulty pattern |
| Slug | — | Low % | — | Inconsistent results ⚠️ Exact data unclear *(B Team as of 2026-03-21; historical data)* |
| Peej | — | 28.6% | 1.24 | Y11S1: 7M / 28.6% / K/D 1.24 — Weak WR despite positive K/D — team-wide weakness |

**Notes:** Nighthaven Labs is extremely polarizing. Smigs and Sarge excel (vertical positioning, exterior play), while Grant and Hound struggle significantly. This suggests the map rewards specific play patterns or positioning skills. Two strategies developed but high variance in execution. Recommend analysis of Smigs/Sarge vertical setup vs. Grant/Hound approach to identify playable strategies for entire team.

---

*Confidence: ✅ Medium-High — Verified via Rainbow Six Wiki (Operation Solar Raid, Y7S4 2022). Layout confirmed: Basement, 1F, 2F, Rooftop. Core callout names verified (Server, Garage, Electrical, Control, Kitchen, Vending, Cargo, Exo are standard competitive callouts). Map location Singapore verified. Wiki indicates "many entry points and breach points" supporting existing KB's emphasis on exterior/window play. Recommend verification of: basement layout complexity, exact exterior zone callouts, and secondary room naming conventions. Flag with ⚠️: Zipline feature existence, Research/Lab basement area separation, multi-corridor routing details.*

strats.content:
# Oregon — Main Dorms Hall / Kids Dormitory (2F) · ATTACK

**Status:** ⚠️ Partially Developed (migrated from STACK_03 2026-03-14)
**Formation:** TBD
**Developed:** 2026-03-14 (migrated from STACK_03 inline content)

> **Site Context:** Main Dorms/Kids Dorm is the 2F bomb site. Multiple windows create significant external pressure. Attic connection provides rooftop/exterior approach vulnerability. Iconic spawn peek vulnerability documented. This site is frequently attacked; defenders hold it strongly.

---

## Spawn Peek Risk Warning

⚠️ **CRITICAL:** Attic windows are a primary spawn peek threat. Grant died from spawn peek during strat development. **Approach this site assuming Attic hold is defended.** Early utility (smokes, flashes) essential for safety on approach. Do not rely on speed; use utility to suppress Attic/window positions before advancing.

---

## Spawn

_TBD_ — Spawn selection not specified in source. Select spawn enabling safe approach to site given spawn peek risk.

---

## Roles

| Player | Role | Operator | Assignment |
|--------|------|----------|-----------|
| **All 5** | Rush | _TBD_ | Full team coordinated push into site. Note: source states "All 5: Rush" — interpret as aggressive coordinated entry, not individual aggression. |

**Source Note:** Strat development indicates simple "rush" execute — all 5 players coordinate simultaneous aggressive entry into site. Exact operator assignments and role differentiation _TBD_.

**Operator Assignments:** ⚠️ **ALL assignments marked TBD** — Source provides only "rush" designation. Assign operators based on:
- Spawn peek counter-utility (smokes, flashes, or stun options)
- Hard breach capability (Thermite/Ace)
- Anti-gadget (Thatcher)
- Site clearing utility

---

## Execute Sequence

> ⚠️ **Incomplete source data.** Below sequence is logically inferred from "rush" designation and spawn peek risk. Flesh out with actual team callouts and timing.

1. **Approach phase** — All 5 players advance together from spawn. **Early utility deployment:** All players throw smokes/flashes at Attic windows and general exterior areas to suppress spawn peek positions. Do not sprint into exposed windows.

2. **Window control** — Establish window control via utility suppression. Identify and suppress Attic/Kids Dorm window holds before main site entry.

3. **Main Dorms/Kids Dorm entry** — All 5 push simultaneously into site. Breach both Main Dorms and Kids Dorm entries (or primary entry, depending on defender setup).

4. **Utility denial** — Hard breach on site walls (if reinforced). Clear hatches/vertical threats. Deny defender gadgets on entry points.

5. **Site takeover** — Full team secures site. Establish crossfire control and deny roamer counter-push.

6. **Plant** — Plant in preferred location with cover. Likely Main Dorms or Kids Dorm depending on defender setup and site control.

---

## Plant Position (PP)

**Primary:** _TBD_ — Likely Main Dorms or Kids Dorm (source does not specify preferred plant spot)
**Alternate:** _TBD_

⚠️ **Mark specific plant spots in-game based on defender setup and window exposure.**

---

## Post-Plant

| Player | Position | Job |
|--------|----------|-----|
| _TBD_ | _TBD_ | _TBD_ |
| _TBD_ | _TBD_ | _TBD_ |
| _TBD_ | _TBD_ | _TBD_ |
| _TBD_ | _TBD_ | _TBD_ |
| _TBD_ | _TBD_ | _TBD_ |

---

## Watch For

- **Attic spawn peek** — Primary documented threat. Window positions must be suppressed before main entry. If spawn peek occurs, respond with utility and immediate site rush to overwhelm defenders.

- **Attic roamer pressure** — Defenders may hold Attic window position aggressively to catch pushers. Early utility suppression (smokes, flashes) followed by immediate site push minimizes roamer impact.

- **Hallway roamer** — Secondary roamer may hold Hallway junction. Account for this during main entry.

- **Kids Dorm roamer flank** — Defenders may position roamer in Kids Dorm to flank Main Dorms push. Clear Kids Dorm simultaneously with Main Dorms entry to prevent flank.

- **Bathroom roamer** — Alternative roamer position. Identify during approach.

---

## Win Conditions

### Win A — Primary: Spawn Peek Suppressed, Full Site Rush ✅
**When:** All 5 throw early utility at Attic/windows, defenders cannot spawn peek, site entry succeeds.

Execute full team simultaneous push. Suppress Attic positions with utility. Rush site. Plant. Win.

**Who calls it:** IGL confirms Attic suppressed and clears team to execute.

---

### Win B — Spawn Peek Occurs, Adapt to Gunfight ⚠️
**When:** Early spawn peek kills a player despite utility, defenders get picks on approach.

**Adjustment:**
- Do not abandon execute. Adapt to reduced numbers (5v4 or worse).
- Fall back to perimeter hold if heavily disadvantaged.
- Wait for utility refresh if available.
- Re-position around cover and re-engage from different angle.
- Prioritize not dying to spawn peeks — minimize wasted rounds.

**Who calls it:** IGL identifies lost players and calls adjusted approach.

---

### Win C — Site Denied, Fall Back to Perimeter ⚠️
**When:** Hard defenders lock site, roamers push early, team cannot breach main doors/walls.

**Adjustment:**
- Fall back to perimeter hold outside site.
- Establish spawn control.
- Wait for utility refresh (grenades, smokes).
- Re-engage from stronger position.
- Do not waste utility trying to force a denied entry.

---

## Audibles

- **Attic spawn peek confirmed:** Respond with targeted utility. IGL calls "Attic suppressed" before team rushes.

- **Unexpected roamer early:** Fall back to perimeter. Re-position and re-engage.

- **Multiple defenders on windows:** Adjust utility allocation — focus smokes on deadliest window positions first.

---

## Development Notes & TODOs

⚠️ **This strategy is incomplete and requires team refinement:**

- [ ] Define specific operator selections for each role (currently TBD)
- [ ] Flesh out Post-Plant positioning and holdout angles
- [ ] Define concrete spawn peek mitigation timing and utility allocation
- [ ] Specify plant locations (Main Dorms vs. Kids Dorm preference)
- [ ] Define specific Win Conditions and audibles based on actual team practice
- [ ] Record actual execute sequence timing from team practice
- [ ] Test spawn peek suppression effectiveness with actual utility combinations
- [ ] Document roamer threat areas and how team will clear them during rush

**Next Steps:** Migrate this strategy to team practice with emphasis on spawn peek suppression. Record actual timings, utility effectiveness, and roamer positioning. Refine plant spots and post-plant holds based on live play.

---

_Last migrated: 2026-03-14 from STACK_03 inline strat. Marked as partially developed pending team refinement. Note: Grant died from spawn peek during strat dev — suggests strat needs spawn peek suppression refinement._

overviewContent:
# Oregon — Overview

⚠️ **Y11S1 REWORK NOTICE:** Oregon was modernized in Operation Silent Hunt (Y11S1). Room names and layout below are based on verified callouts and floor plan images. Verify in-game before relying in match.

**Environment:** Classic militia compound; rustic house with barn structure and underground utility areas
**Floors:** Basement / Underground (B), Ground Floor (1F), Upper Floor / Attic (2F)
**In Y11S1 Ranked Pool:** ✅ Yes — first half of season only. Exits at Y11S1 mid-season swap (replaced by undisclosed maps).
**Year Added / Reworked:** Original map (Y1S0); major rework Operation Void Edge (Y5S2, March 2020); Y11S1 modernization rework

---

## Overview

Oregon is a classic competitive map featuring a main residential building with attached barn/utility structure and basement areas. The tight, close-quarters layout favors defenders with well-coordinated positioning and roaming. Multiple soft walls enable flexible rotations and site setup variations. The map's iconic design features distinct elevation changes, tight hallways, and limited long sightlines. Attackers require methodical clear operations and coordinated site execution.

Oregon has been a strong performer for the stack historically, with positive win rates across most players. However, **Grant's recent priorities flag Oregon as a WEAK map this season (6M/33.3% WR).** Despite the Y10S4 strong historical record, the current team performance has deteriorated. **Do NOT auto-protect Oregon in bans.** Reassess ban strategy based on current meta and team comfort.

One partial strategy developed (likely ATK_Attic_Bedroom from STACK_03 inline content, noting spawn peek vulnerability and Grant death during strat dev). Three bomb sites remain underdeveloped. Recommend documenting strategies with emphasis on Attic spawn peek mitigations.

---

## Bomb Sites

### Site 1 — Main Dorms Hall / Kids Dormitory — 2F
**Rooms:** Main Dorms Hall (1A — main dormitory) / Kids Dormitory (1B — children's bedroom)
**Description:** Upper floor site with iconic spawn peek vulnerability documented in strat dev. Main Dorms Hall is spacious with multiple beds and furniture providing cover; Kids Dormitory is smaller bedroom area. Multiple windows create significant external pressure. Attic connection provides rooftop/exterior approach vulnerability. Site is frequently attacked and heavily defended against.
**Common defense:** One anchor holds interior Main Dorms, with dedicated roamer holding Kids Dorm/Attic area for potential spawn peeks. Multiple soft wall rotations enable aggressive defender positioning. Window defense critical.
**Attack approach:** Account for spawn peek threat early in approach. Clear Kids Dorm/Attic carefully; prioritize window control before interior breach. Main Dorms Hall breach requires soft wall clearing and utility denial. Use utility to suppress defenders from window positions.
**⚠️ Spawn Peek Risk:** Documented vulnerability in existing partial strat. Grant died from spawn peek during strat development — flag this site as having spawn peek risk. Early utility (smokes, flashes) essential for approach safety.

### Site 2 — Dining Hall / Kitchen — 1F
**Rooms:** Dining Hall (2A — dining area) / Kitchen (2B — shared utility area)
**Description:** Ground floor site featuring dining and food preparation areas. Dining Hall is moderately open with dining furniture; Kitchen offers utility storage and tight angles. Central location with good rotation access through soft walls. Moderate attacker commitment required. Shared Kitchen area with alternate Meeting Hall site (Site 3).
**Common defense:** Anchor typically holds Kitchen for tight angles; one roamer plays adjacent Laundry/Bathroom for rotation denial. Soft wall reinforcement variable depending on site defense strategy and attacker location prediction.
**Attack approach:** Clear adjacent Laundry/Bathroom first to deny roamer flanks. Breach both Dining and Kitchen simultaneously or in quick succession. Multiple entry points from hallways and exterior access. Exterior breaches possible via barn/Watch Tower area.

### Site 3 — Meeting Hall / Kitchen — 1F
**Rooms:** Meeting Hall (3A — meeting/operations room) / Kitchen (3B — shared utility area)
**Description:** Alternate 1F bomb site pairing using shared Kitchen area with alternate primary room. Meeting Hall is a tactical operations/briefing room with different defensive angles than Dining Hall. Creates significant setup flexibility — both 1F sites share Kitchen but have different primary rooms. Attacker difficulty: early drone identification is critical to determine which 1F site is hit.
**Common defense:** Can anchor in Kitchen for either 1F site; Meeting Hall offers distinct defensive angles compared to Dining Hall. Roamer coverage varies based on which site is hit. Kitchen remains central defensive position.
**Attack approach:** Early drone identification critical to determine which 1F site is hit. Approach varies significantly — Dining Hall requires Laundry approach while Meeting Hall has different corridor vectors. Kitchen clearing must be flexible for both site possibilities.

### Site 4 — Laundry Room / Supply Room — Basement
**Rooms:** Laundry Room (4A — laundry/utility) / Supply Room (4B — supply storage)
**Description:** Basement level site with tight corridors and limited visibility. Laundry Room offers utility focus; Supply Room is storage-focused. Underground positioning makes defender rotations to upper floors time-consuming and resource-intensive. Multiple hatch access from above creates strong vertical vulnerability.
**Common defense:** Anchor holds basement area, typically consolidates between Laundry/Supply rooms. Roamer plays upper floor basement stairs/entry points for vertical pressure denial. Communication critical due to vertical isolation.
**Attack approach:** Vertical pressure from above is major advantage — hatches should be primary entry vector. Target basement stairs entry early to secure access routes. Coordinate simultaneous hatches with ground level basement stairs approach for split defense. Underground nature enables rapid site takeover if vertical control established.

---

## Room Names & Community Callouts

### 2F (Upper Floor / Attic) — Verified
| Room | Callout | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| Main Dorms Hall | Main Dorms / Dorms | Bomb site (1A); upper floor dormitory; spawn peek vulnerability |
| Kids Dormitory | Kids / Kids Dorm | Bomb site (1B); children's bedroom; adjacent to Main Dorms |
| Attic | Attic | Upper floor storage; exposed windows; CRITICAL spawn peek position |
| Hallway | Hall / Hallway / 2F Hall | Main 2F circulation corridor |
| Bathroom | Bathroom | Utility room; central to multiple areas |
| Stairs | Stairs / Main Stairs | Primary vertical access to 1F |

### 1F (Ground Floor) — Verified
| Room | Callout | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| Dining Hall | Dining / Dining Room | Bomb site (2A); primary dining area |
| Meeting Hall | Meeting / Meeting Room | Bomb site (3A); alternate 1F site; meeting/operations room |
| Kitchen | Kitchen / Kit | Bomb site (2B, 3B); shared between both 1F sites; central utility |
| Laundry | Laundry / Wash | Common roamer position; 1F utility area |
| Bathroom | Bathroom | Utility restroom; central to Kitchen/Dining area |
| Living Area | Living / Main | Central living space; circulation hub |
| Hallway | Hall / Main Hall | Central circulation corridor |
| Basement Stairs | Basement Stairs / Down | Vertical descent to Basement; tight chokepoint |
| Exterior (barn area) | Barn / Outside | Attached barn structure; exterior pressure |
| Watch Tower | Watch Tower / Tower | Exterior tower structure; rappel access |

### Basement / Underground (B) — Floor Plan Labeled
| Room | Callout | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| Laundry Room | Laundry / Wash | Bomb site (4A); basement laundry/utility |
| Supply Room | Supply | Bomb site (4B); supply storage; hatch from Kitchen above |
| Basement Hall / Basement Corridor | Basement / Base Corridor | Main basement circulation |
| Utility Area | Utility / Utilities / Electric | Support systems area |
| Storage | Storage | Equipment storage basement area |
| Back Stairs | Back Stairs | Rear stairwell; basement to exterior |

⚠️ **Floor Plan Basement Labels:** Basement image shows many utility area names (Pillar, Boiler, Electric, Bunker, Barrels, Closet, Back Laundry, Laundry Storage, Freezer, Freezer Stairs). Reconcile these floor plan labels against verified in-game callouts — Y11S1 rework may have changed naming.

### Exterior / Attacker Spawns
| Location | Callout | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Main front | Front / Outside | Primary exterior entrance |
| Back area | Back / Rear | Rear of main building |
| Barn exterior | Barn | Attached barn/utility structure |
| Watch Tower | Watch Tower / Tower | Tall external structure with rappel points |
| Side areas | Side / Left / Right | Building sides |

---

## Key Walls & Hatches

### Critical Reinforced Walls
- **Attic / Bedroom wall (2F):** Typically reinforced to separate objectives; controls sight lines between rooms
- **Kitchen / Dining connecting wall (1F):** Variable reinforcement — often left soft for roamer rotation flexibility
- **Kitchen / Meeting connecting wall (1F):** Similar reinforcement strategy; depends on which 1F site is hit
- **Basement stair entry wall (1F → B):** Reinforced to deny direct vertical line of sight and slow basement entry
- **Laundry / Supply division wall (Basement):** Reinforced to create defensive compartments

### Key Soft Walls
- **Hallway walls (all floors):** Multiple soft wall options enabling roamer rotation and attacker breach points
- **Laundry walls (1F):** Soft walls create roamer movement flexibility and attacker site isolation
- **Bathroom walls (1F/2F):** Multiple soft options for rotation enhancement
- **Basement corridor walls (B):** Soft walls enable multi-vector basement approaches

### Key Hatches
- **2F to 1F hatch (main stairwell):** Primary vertical connection; contested hatch position
- **1F to Basement hatch (Kitchen area):** Direct basement access; critical for vertical pressure coordination
- **Additional basement hatches:** Floor plan shows multiple connections; verify exact locations in-game

---

## Stack Performance Notes (Y11S1)

⚠️ **Roster change 2026-03-21:** Peej promoted to main stack; Slug moved to B Team. Slug's historical data retained for reference. Peej's Oregon performance data pending.

| Player | Y10S4 WR | Y11S1 Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grant** | 71.4% | **⚠️ 33.3% (6M)** | **WEAK MAP THIS SEASON.** Dramatic performance drop. Do NOT auto-protect. |
| **Smigs** | 71.4% | _TBD_ | Historically strong; verify current status |
| **Peej** | _TBD_ | _TBD_ | New to main stack 2026-03-21; no prior Oregon data |
| **Slug** | 60.9% | _N/A_ | *B Team as of 2026-03-21; historical data retained for reference* |
| **Sarge** | _Unclear_ | _TBD_ | Data unclear; verify current status |

**Y11S1 Ban Strategy:** Oregon historically strong, but **Grant's 33.3% WR this season is a major red flag.** Despite team strength on this map, current meta or team comfort has deteriorated. **Do NOT assume Oregon is a protect/keep map.** Reassess based on:
- Root cause analysis of Grant's poor performance
- Team's overall performance trajectory on Oregon this season
- Current meta changes affecting map viability
- Recommend monitoring performance and adjusting ban strategy accordingly

---

## Y11S1 Pool Status

**In Rotation:** ✅ Yes — first half of season only
**Exit Timing:** Mid-season swap (Y11S1 replaced maps at midpoint)
**Ban Priority:** ⚠️ Do NOT auto-protect — reassess current season performance before including in protect/ban strategy

---

_Confidence: ✅ High for 2F (Main Dorms/Kids Dorm) and 1F (Dining/Kitchen/Meeting) bomb sites — community standard callouts verified. ⚠️ Medium-High for Basement layout — floor plan labels visible but must be verified against Y11S1 rework updates. Recommend in-game verification of basement room naming, hatch positions, and any layout changes before competitive play. High confidence in core map fundamentals but recommend current-season performance re-evaluation before making protective/ban decisions._
referenceContent:
# Oregon — Map Reference

⚠️ **CRITICAL VERIFICATION NOTE:** Oregon was reworked in Y11S1 (Operation Silent Hunt). Room names and layout on this reference are extracted from the updated floor plan images. Verify callout names in-game before relying on this document in a match.

**Source:** In-game labeled floor plan images (Map Images folder — Basement, Ground Floor, Top Floor)
**Last Updated:** 2026-03-14 (extracted from floor plan images post Y11S1 rework)

---

## Bomb Sites

| Site | Room A | Room B | Floor |
|------|--------|--------|-------|

Unique vertical plays. ⚠️ Verify Attic accessibility and window positions in-game. |

Meeting/operations room — distinct from Dining Hall defensive angles. ⚠️ Verify Meeting Hall existence and adjacency in Y11S1 rework. |

## Basement Layout

These floor plan labels should be verified against in-game callouts. Current Oregon.md uses "Laundry Room / Supply Room" as bomb sites; verify these room names match Y11S1 floor plan labeling.

---

## Key Adjacencies by Bomb Site

### Site 1 — Main Dorms Hall / Kids Dormitory (2F)
**Attacker approach rooms:** Hallway (→ Main Dorms or Kids Dorm entry), Attic (exterior roamer threat), exterior pressure via windows
**Defender roam territory:** Attic (critical roamer position — spawn peek and exterior denial), Hallway, Bathroom
**Vertical access:** Main Stairs from 1F (up to Hallway), hatches on 2F floor enable vertical pressure from below
**Spawn peek vulnerability:** Attic windows primary threat — documented in existing partial strat. Critical to account for early in approach.

### Site 2 — Dining Hall / Kitchen (1F)
**Attacker approach rooms:** Hallway (→ Dining entry), Kitchen (interior breach), Laundry (→ Kitchen flank), Bathroom (→ site pressure)
**Defender roam territory:** Laundry, Bathroom, Hallway, Living area
**Vertical access:** Basement Stairs (down from Hallway area into Basement), hatch access from Kitchen area to Basement
**Note:** Shared Kitchen with Site 3 pairing — attackers must identify which 1F site is being hit.

### Site 3 — Meeting Hall / Kitchen (1F)
**Attacker approach rooms:** Hallway (→ Meeting entry), Kitchen (interior breach), similar to Dining/Kitchen approach
**Defender roam territory:** Similar to Dining — Laundry, Bathroom, Hallway
**Vertical access:** Same Basement Stairs and Kitchen hatch as Dining/Kitchen pairing
**Note:** Alternate 1F site using shared Kitchen. Distinct defensive angles from Dining Hall but same utility areas.

### Site 4 — Laundry Room / Supply Room (Basement)
**Attacker approach rooms:** Basement Stairs (→ Laundry), Basement Corridor, Supply Room (internal)
**Defender roam territory:** Basement Corridor, Utility area, 1F Basement Stairs area
**Vertical access:** Hatch from 1F Kitchen area (above Supply Room). Basement Stairs as tight chokepoint.
**Isolation:** Underground positioning — limited 1F roamer support. Vertical pressure from above is major attacker advantage.

---

## Vertical Relationship (1F → 2F)

## Basement to 1F Vertical Connections

| Supply Room | Kitchen hatch (ceiling destruction from above) | Vertical pressure point |

## Staircase Reference

## Hatch Reference

These labels must be reconciled with verified in-game room names, especially post Y11S1 rework.

---

## Y11S1 Rework Changes

⚠️ Oregon was modernized in Operation Silent Hunt (Y11S1). Possible changes include:
- Room layout adjustments
- Hatch position changes
- Soft wall placement modifications
- Callout name updates

**All references in this document should be verified in-game before relying in competition.** Cross-reference floor plan images against actual in-game map to confirm:

## Name Change Log

No comprehensive name changes documented. Oregon has stable callouts since Void Edge rework (Y5S2, March 2020). However, Y11S1 may have introduced new callout names or room adjustments.
